did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a self-modifying meta-learning method (combining meta-learning and curriculum learning) based on a convolutional neural network (CNN) to perform skin cancer classification and preliminary diagnosis. The model was evaluated on the ISIC 2018 skin lesion classification dataset, achieving 79.2% accuracy with 5 samples per class and 76.2% accuracy on unseen classes with noisy data.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a 'self-modifying meta-learning method' for skin cancer classification. It includes specific sections for Methods (describing the algorithm and dataset), Results (reporting accuracy metrics on the ISIC 2018 dataset with clean and noisy data), and Discussion. Although published in a conference proceedings (ICBASE), it reports new algorithmic results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: An auxiliary tool for preliminary tests of skin cancer A self-modifying meta-learning method for clean and noisy data
journal: 2021 2nd International Conference on Big Data & Artificial Intelligence & Software Engineering (ICBASE)
brief_summary: This study proposes a self-modifying meta-learning model combining meta-learning and curriculum learning to address skin cancer classification, particularly for rare diseases and noisy data. The method utilizes a convolutional neural network trained on the ISIC 2018 skin lesion dataset, which contains images from seven skin disease categories. The evaluation approach involved few-shot learning scenarios using only five samples per class and testing robustness against 20% label noise. The model achieved an accuracy of 79.2% on clean data with five samples per class and 76.2% accuracy on unseen classes with noisy data. These results demonstrate the model's effectiveness in low-data regimes and its potential as an auxiliary tool for preliminary skin cancer diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: Included in the ISIC 2018 dataset with 6705 images; used as a common disease (meta-train dataset) in the experiment.
Included in the ISIC 2018 dataset with 1113 images; used as a common disease (meta-train dataset) in the experiment.
Included in the ISIC 2018 dataset with 1099 images; used as a common disease (meta-train dataset) in the experiment.
Included in the ISIC 2018 dataset with 514 images; used as a validation set class in the experiment.
Included in the ISIC 2018 dataset with 327 images; used as a validation set class in the experiment.
Included in the ISIC 2018 dataset with 142 images; used as a rare disease (meta-test dataset) in the experiment.
Included in the ISIC 2018 dataset with 115 images; used as a rare disease (meta-test dataset) in the experiment.

all_task_types.target: Skin cancer diagnostic categories (e.g., melanocytic nevus, melanoma, benign keratosis, basal cell carcinoma, actinic keratosis, vascular lesion, dermatofibroma)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs skin lesion classification using a self-modifying meta-learning method. The task involves categorizing skin images into specific disease classes. The study evaluates binary classification tasks during meta-training and multi-class classification during meta-testing on the ISIC 2018 dataset. Performance is measured by accuracy (79.2% on clean data, 76.2% on noisy unseen classes).

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018 Skin Lesion Analysis Towards Melanoma Detection Dataset
datasets_used.description: A public dataset containing skin lesion images from seven skin diseases, used for meta-training, validation, and meta-testing in a few-shot learning framework.

use.training: Used as the meta-train dataset consisting of the three classes with the largest number of cases (melanocytic nevus, melanoma, benign keratosis) for binary classification tasks during meta-training.
use.validation: Used as the validation set consisting of two classes with a modest number of cases (basal cell carcinoma, actinic keratosis) for calculating losses during meta-training.
use.testing: Used as the meta-test dataset consisting of the two classes with the least number of cases (vascular lesion, dermatofibroma) for evaluating performance on unseen classes.
use.other: Noise was artificially added to the dataset (20% label noise) to test the model's robustness.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the performance of the self-modifying meta-learning model on clean and noisy data compared to baseline methods (MAML, DAML).
To report the variance or stability of the accuracy results for the proposed method and baselines across different noise levels.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy and standard deviation across different noise levels and compared against baseline models (MAML, DAML) without statistical significance testing.

clean_data.notes: Results obtained using only 5 samples per class. The proposed method achieved the best accuracy within 2000 iterations.

noisy_data_0.4.notes: Noise rate of 0.4 applied to the dataset. This corresponds to an expectation of 20% noisy data rate in the training set (randomly chosen labels).

noisy_data_0.8.notes: Noise rate of 0.8 applied to the dataset.

architecture_used.main_model: Self-modifying meta-learning model (combining meta-learning and curriculum learning) based on a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN)

architecture_details.methodology: Two-order optimization meta-learning combining supervised learning and meta-learning. The model uses a major model with parameter and a weighted network with parameter w. It samples instances from a probability distribution to update parameters via cross-entropy loss for binary classification tasks. The major model is updated with both the newly generated weighted network parameter and its original value.

training_details.dataset: ISIC 2018 Skin Lesion Analysis Towards Melanoma Detection Dataset
training_details.iterations: Up to 10,000 iterations (results reported up to 2,000 iterations for best accuracy)
training_details.noise_simulation: 20% label noise (randomly chosen labels) added to training data to test robustness
training_details.task_type: Binary classification during meta-training; multi-class classification during meta-testing

classification_or_output_head.type: Softmax/Classification head implied by cross-entropy loss and accuracy metric
classification_or_output_head.classes: 7 classes (melanocytic nevus, melanoma, benign keratosis, basal cell carcinoma, actinic keratosis, vascular lesion, dermatofibroma)

preprocessing_techniques: Artificial label noise injection (20% noise rate via random label assignment) to simulate noisy data and test model robustness

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Noise was added to the training dataset to test robustness. Specifically, there was a 50% chance that a batch would be selected for noise injection. Within those batches, each data point had a probability of 'noisy rate' (0.4 or 0.8 in experiments, corresponding to an expectation of 20% noisy data rate in the training set) of having its label randomly reassigned to a different class.

conclusions: The proposed self-modifying meta-learning model, which combines meta-learning and curriculum learning, achieves high accuracy (79.1% on clean data and 76.2% on data with 20% label noise) using only 5 samples per class, demonstrating its effectiveness in few-shot learning scenarios for skin cancer classification.

The model demonstrates strong robustness against noisy data, outperforming baseline methods (MAML and DAML) across different noise levels (0.4 and 0.8 noise rates), making it suitable for real-world datasets with manual labeling errors.

The method addresses the challenge of rare diseases and insufficient data by training on common diseases and adapting to rare ones, offering a practical solution for preliminary skin cancer diagnosis where large datasets are unavailable.

The model serves as an auxiliary tool for medical workers and ordinary users, helping to reduce the burden on doctors by handling preliminary screening tasks and providing basic knowledge about skin conditions.

A key limitation acknowledged is that the few-shot learning approach leads to insufficient training data compared to traditional deep learning models, resulting in lower diagnostic accuracy than models trained on massive datasets.

Future work aims to improve accuracy through multi-dataset training, modifying the model structure for unseen data, incorporating multi-modal medical materials (e.g., cell images, pathological sections), and applying ensemble learning to enhance classification performance.
important_remarks: The study is limited to a few-shot learning scenario (5 samples per class), which inherently restricts the model's ability to acquire adequate information compared to traditional deep learning models trained on massive datasets, resulting in lower diagnostic accuracy.
The model was evaluated exclusively on the ISIC 2018 dataset; there is no external validation on independent datasets or diverse skin-of-color populations, limiting generalizability.

The paper acknowledges that the current implementation focuses only on preliminary skin cancer classification and does not address other critical aspects of real-world clinical application, such as multi-modal data integration (e.g., cell images, pathological sections) or ensemble learning.
Robustness was tested using artificially injected label noise (20% rate) rather than natural image noise or domain shifts, which may not fully reflect real-world clinical data quality issues.

The paper does not report statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) for the performance differences between the proposed method and baselines (MAML, DAML), relying solely on accuracy and standard deviation.

The proposed tool is explicitly framed as an 'auxiliary' tool for preliminary screening to reduce doctor burden, not as a definitive diagnostic system, highlighting its role in triage rather than final diagnosis.
